description: Defining Social Change: Patterns of change, factors and conditions of change, direction of change, control of change, planned change. Relevant concepts:Progress, Urbanization,industrialization, modernization, Developemnt, Revolution and Social movements as forms of change. Theories of change: Modernization theory and conflict theory. India"s response to modernity: Relations between tradition and modernity (antagonistic or continuity and persistence, modernization of tradition and traditionalization of modernity.) Theories of under-development/dependency, Relations between planning and development, social factors of economic planning and development. Culture of development: Role of culture in social developemnt values and development congruence, Indian values and behaviour dispositions in the context of National development, Reorganizing values for development,Designing organizational culture and behaviour tools for development. Psychology of developing societies: Psychology of poverty and deprivation, perspectives on organizational alleviation of poverty and deprivation, psychology of rural poor. Role of strategic organizations (including bureaucracy) in planning and development, Re-vitalization of sick strategic organizations.

Empirical assessment of the inter-linkages between environmental quality and economic development. Comparative experiences of developed and developing countries. Environmental problems and policies in India. Environmental issues in the Global Context: GHG and Ozone Depletion, International protocols, global trade and Environmental standards and WTO.

Humn resources development through Indian planning:Past experience and future challenges-Ethnic issues, illiteracy, social and protective discrimination. Motivation, training and development, performance appraisal, and leadership issues. Organizational change and development strategies for macro level.

Areas of Development Administration: Regional Planning and Developmental Administration: Urban and Rural Development Programs role of local bodies, including voluntary organisations. Structure of leadership and Development Adminstration in Regional Development. Plannig, Evaluation and Organisation for Developmental Admisnstration: Project Appraisals; Techniques of evaluation; Financial and organisation problems-Role of Banks and Co-operatives. Attitude and Motivational aspects of Bureaucrats, Planners, Politicians:Leadership Training; Training in Development dministration; Management and Training for entrepreneural Development.

title: Computer Aided Applied Statistics
description: The course will provide an overview of univariate and multivariate descriptive and inferential statistics, and give an outline of logical and procedural aspects of the application of statistical methods for data reduction and hypothesis testing in social science research. The focus will be on how the concepts and tools are of statistics are used in the analysis rather than on the theorems and derivative aspects. The course will emphasise the use of computer software with special reference to SPSS software routines and sub-routines. Accordingly, the course will be computer-lab based. Statistics and the research process, Scientific methods, the role of statistics in the study of behaviour. Data displays, frequency distributions and graphical analysis. Measures of central tendency and dispersion. Probability, the theory of statistical distributions, normal curve. The theory of statistical inference: Parametric and non-parametric tests, point and interval estimation. Measures of Association: Nominal, Ordinal and Interval level. Correlation and regression. Multivariate analysis. Introduction to factor, cluster and discriminent analysis,Measurement of growth, productivity, well being and deprivation.

title: Language,Develop. & Planning: A Socio -linguistic Perspective

Important contributions of Indians to the science of Mathematics and Astronomy. Division of time - year, month, fortnight, week, days - larger units like yuga, mahÄyuga, manvantara, and kalpa. Need for numerals â€“ different systems adopted for representing numbers - KatapayÄdi, BhÅ«tasankhyÄ and AryabhatÄ«yapaddhati. Introduction to celestial co-ordinate systems, elementary properties of spherical triangle, RaÅ›i and Naksatra divisions of the ecliptic - Indian calendar system (PaÃ±cÄnga). Calculation of Ahargan. a and mean motion of planets. Finding the direction, time and place from shadow measurements (simple experiments). Computation of longitudes and eclipses.

title: Linguistics and Natural Language Processing
description: Aims: To introduce students to the theoretical issues in the mental representation of grammar and natural language processing. The course will address the following questions: What is language? What is universal grammar? What is the internal or mental representation of natural languages? How do languages vary from each other and how are they similar? What is NLP? What is the history of NLP, its current applications and topics? What is the need for precise language understanding? What are the techniques used in NLP? Course Outline: Universal Grammar and mental modules Word building Phrase construction Construing meaning Language variation and invariables NLP Parsing: top-down parsing, bottom-up parsing; n-gram models of language: Relative Frequency estimation from corpora; Markov models, relative entropy, cross entropy; Probabilistic parsing; CYK algorithm; Part of Speech Tagging; Named Entity Tagging; Machine Translation; Information Extraction; Indian Language Computing.

title: Contemporary Indian Philosophy
description: This course will introduce the student to the philosophical contentions, ethical and political arguments and conceptions of the problems of life underlying the writings of a selected list of thinkers of twentieth century India. Together, these streams of thought will also show to the student how the Indian culture and philosophy has grappled with perennial questions in the light of contemporary problems and how questions of life and meaning can be addressed in these times in ways unique to this tradition of philosophical enquiry. The select list of thinkers to be introduced in the course includes: Swami Vivekananda, Rabindranath Tagore, Mahatma Gandhi, Sri Aurobindo, Krishnachandra Bhattacharya, S. Radhakrishnan, Sri Mohammad Iqubal, Jiddu Krishnamurti. The issues to be discussed relating to the following topics is as follows: The concept of Action, Self realization. The Doctrine of Mâyâ. Destiny of the soul. Creation. Non-violence. The technique of Ahimsâ, Satyâgraha. Swaraj. Religion and Morality. The world process. Nature of man, rebirth and law of karma, the seven fold Ignorance. The Supermind. Gnostic Being and Divine Life. Integral Yoga. Philosophy of Spirit, Truth. Notion of Subjectivity. Realization of the Subject’s freedom. The concept of the Absolute Knowledge, Fear and Pleasure, Meditation, Life and Freedom.

title: Problems in Social Ethics
description: Life Ethics: Just War Theory, Killing, Death Penalty, Suicide, Abortion, Euthanasia Social Ethics: Mass Media Ethics, Justice and Democracy, Inclusive Globalization and Global Ethics, Ethics of Development, Equality and Social Differences, Protective Discrimination Debate, Social Responsibility, â€˜What Do I Owe to the Other Person?â€™ Technology Ethics: Ethics of the Cyberspace, Research Ethics, Technology and Humanization of the Planet, Technology and Development, the Appropriate Technology Debate.

title: Contemporary Issues in Philosophy of Mind
description: Mind-Body problem: Interactionism, Instrumentalism, Physicalism, Epiphenomenalism, Dualism, Parallelism, Materialism, Non-Materialistic view of mind, Concept of person/personal identity. Minds and Machines: Functionalism, Computationalism: Multiple Draft Model, Language of Thought, Syntax as Physical Symbol System, Meaning and Mind, Connectionism and Folk-psychology, Eliminative Materialism, Cognitive Psychology, Artificial Intelligence and Creativity, The Non-computational/mechanical Mind: Revisiting the Cartesian theory of the Mind, Phenomenal Consciousness, Subjectivity and Personal Identity, Chalmers Phenomenal mind, Emergentism-Supervenience, Creativity and Cognition, Creativity and Consciousness.

title: Philosophy of Education
description: Education is about learning. Learning is an important aspect of life. Every living being learns to survive. As Charles Darwin hypothesized about the existence of various species in the world that a living being struggles to exist. And whoever is biologically fit will exists otherwise would perish from the world. Following this Karl Popper, one of the eminent philosophers of science advocates evolutionary epistemology. Learning defined in the theoretical framework of evolutionary epistemology shows the knowledge acquisition happens due to certain biological disposition. The biological need of life explains the necessity of knowledge. Learning therefore remains a continuous activity. One continues to learn in order to remain in the world. So the notion of survival or enduring life goes along with seeking information and bringing change in accord with the information of one’s own well being. The enduring life here refers to an active life where the being participates in the world for its well being. Human life is such that the act of knowledge seeking, which Aristotle defines as the desire to know is something very primitive. It is not merely part of human biological history but also significant for understanding the cultural history. The biological history of human life is significant as it has helped us developing our food habits and medicine. The cultural history tells us understanding the development of values in general and institutions and institutional values in particular. The development of culture is significant because human life is not merely confined to certain biological activities. Rather human life transcends the limit of biological existence. This transcendence is embedded in the very attitude of knowledge seeking – learning. Learning pervades both the biological forms of life as well as the cultural forms of life. Interestingly the well being of human life requires both that is the biological fitness and the cultural elegance. In this course, we study philosophical ideas of Plato, Aristotle, Jacque Rousseau, John Dewey, Maria Montessori, Nel Nodding, Paulo Freire, Mahatma Gandhi, Rabindranath Tagore, Sri Aurobindo and Jiddu Krishnamurthi. The teaching will be related to some of the contemporary issues related to education and society.

title: Development of Calculus: Indian and Western Context
description: Prequisite: Knowledge of Devanagari (Hindi) Script Introduction – Sources – Development of ideas of Calculus – Aryabhata’s constuction of sine table – Discussion of irrationality of  – Sum of an infinite geometric series – ´San. kara’s discussion of the binomial series expansion – Archimedes and Integration – Estimation of sums of powers of integers 1 to n for large n. title: Ecology and Society

title: Making Choices : Ethical Decisions in Scientific Endeavour
description: Scholarly integrity is recognized as an important issue in research and training. Yet there are profound differences in the larger cultural context for ethical decision-making. As international collaborations become more common, new generations of scholars enter the field of research, and norms and consequences of research become more complicated, these differences need to be more fully confronted. Standard guidelines on responsible conduct of research or IPR / Copyright rules tend to ignore cross-cultural challenges of ethical decision-making in the scientific endeavor, and also ignore alternate perspectives and approaches to such issues. Moving away from norms that are largely proscriptive, field- and country-specific, and from guidelines and approaches to ethical issues that are universalizing, or based on fundamentally western notions of individual intellectual property rights rather than collaborative values, this course places emphasis upon cultural context and upon partnership with international scholars and diverse community groups in developing a new kind of training in scholarly integrity. Through discussing a range of topics from science and engineering especially related to issues of development and change, the course aims to develop skills related to decisions making in the scientific endeavour – from issues of methodology and development paradigms, to issues of responsibility, informed consent, intellectual property rights, commons, natural resources, livelihoods, sustainability, participation, and scientific and engineering ethics codes. Case studies from real life will be used as tools for class room based workshops on ethical decision making in different scientific arenas. Contentious issues from areas such as biotechnology, civil engineering, water resources management, IPRs/creative commons, and environmental risks will form the core of issues around which teaching will be organized. Topics to be covered1. Introduction: What is ethics? What is morality? What is responsibility?2. Methodology, Epistemology and Ethics: Positivism and its critiques, nature of data, qualitative vs quantitative research3. Thinking about Development: choices and paradigms4. Intellectual Property and Property Rights; Open Access and Creative Commons5. Cultural Competence, Cultural Humility and Participatory Research6. National and International Science and Engineering Ethics Codes7. Ethics and Natural resources (water, forestry, minerals)8. Ethics of Planning and Policy Making9. Ethics of Risk and UncertaintyTwo workshop sessions on Ethics in Agricultural Biotechnology and WaterModes of Assessment1. Mid-semester and End-semester examination (weightage: 70%)2. Classroom participation, presentations, and discussion (10%)3. Assignment: Development of Ethics Case Studies (10%)4. Workshop participation and presentation (10%)Instruction: There will be extensive use of case studies. Students are requested to come prepared and read the material to be uploaded on moodle before every class. All course material will be made available through moodle.

title: Economtrics of Programme Evaluation
description: 1.Problem of Causal Inference and Randomized Experiments: Cause and effects, randomized and non-randomized trials, treatment effect and selection bias 2.Regression Fundamentals and Causality: Introduction to regression analysis, ordinary least squares regression, linear regression and conditional expectation, inference and hypothesis testing in regression, regression and causality 3.Matching: Counterfactuals, propensity score matching, average treatment effect, quantile treatment effect, regression and matching, ordered and continuous treatment 4.Instrumental Variables Regression: Instrumental variables and causality, Two-stage least squares, 2SLS inference, IV with heterogeneous potential outcomes 5.Difference-in-difference Estimator: Individual fixed effects, difference-in-difference, regression DD

title: Theories of Verbal Congnition (Shaabdabodha)
description: Discourse Analysis, Anaphora, Cognitive faculties of human being, types of cognition, features of verbal cognition, Significance of Verbal Cognition, Causes of verbal cognition. Nature of Verbal cognition according to Naiyayikas (Logicians), Meaning of the verbal root, meaning of suffixes, Simple sentence and complex sentences, problems in this view.Nature of Verbal cognition according to Mimamsakas (Hermeneuticians), concept of vidhi,concept of bhaavanaa, Kumaril302222s view and Prabhakara302222s view, problems in this view. Nature of Verbal cognition according to Vaiyakaranas (Gramamrians), concept of Word Permanancy, Word as a primary meaning of a word, Concept of Word universal. Application of these theories in Natural Language Proceesing of Sanskrit and other Indian Languages.

title: Development of Mathematics in India
description: Introduction; Mathematics in Antiquity; Sulvasutras – Construc- tion of altars, Expression for surds; Mathematics in Jaina and Bauddha texts; Development of the Decimal Place value system. Aryabhata and his contributions – Construction of sine table, Value of Pi, Sum of series, Kuttaka problem; Brahmagupta and his Bha- vana principle; Mathematics during 7–11th centuries. Bhaskara II and his Cakravala Algorithm; Glimpses of Ganitakau- mudi of Narayana Pandita. Contribution of the Kerala School of Mathematics – Discussion on the irrationality of Pi, Sum of an infinite geometric series, Binomial expansion, Estimation of sums of powers of integers 1 to n for large n. Derivation of the Lieb- niz/Madhava series for Pi, Transformation of the series for fast con- vergence, Infinite series for the sine and cosine functions, Deriva- tives of ratios of functions – Justifications and Proofs.

title: Anthropology and Disaster
description: • History of the social science study of disaster • What can anthropology tell us about disaster? • What is a disaster? Types and causes • Differences between hazard, emergency, disaster, catastrophe • Anthropology of disaster: context, community, aftermath, coping, change & adaptation • Social science theories of disaster: behaviorist or functionalist; hazard approach; political economy approaches and the notions of ‘risk’ and ‘vulnerability’ • Disaster and gender, caste, class, race and ethnicity • What can disasters tell us about anthropology?

title: Making of a Man: A Study of Mahatma Gandhi
description: Locating Gandhi as a child: Social, Political and Religious Environment, Early Education Inclination. Temptation and Fear; Power of Resistance and Over Coming confusion. Building of the sacks of Morality: Preliminary Moral Lessons, Birth of Man: Gandhi`s Vows and Journey to London.Gandhi`s Exposure to Western World and his Reflection of the East, Being part of the Vegetarian Club and Reading of the Gita, Cultivation of the Spirit of Morality. Slef Knowledge: Efforts & Preparation of a person, Education: Empowering oneself as a professional.Gandhi as a Professional: Success and Failure, Gandhi`s Preparation as a Leader, Gandhi and Birth of Freedom struggle, Non-violance: Agaist Discrimination and Insjustice, Ashram: Home, Hope and Self 0reliance.Gandhi Returns Home, Gandhi as a great Thinker, Listener and Communicator, Gandhi as an Intellectual: Speaking Truth to Power, social and Economic Development (Education an Health), Searaj an Swadeshi.Gandhi as a Spiritual Person: A Seeker of Truth, prayer, Contemplation and Conscience. Non-violence as a Mantra of Life, End of Life: Quest for Peace and Harmony.

title: Civil Society: Its Foundations and Future
description: Relations between the family and civil society - Plato and Aristotle, Civility and Intellectual Autonomy – Kant, Civil Society as the site of inequality – Rousseau, Origin of Civility in the sentiment of sympathy – Smith, Communism as the future of civility - Marx, The Civil Sphere as a Contemporary Idea – Jeffery, Post-Colonial Democracies and Civil Society – Chatterjee, De-Provincializing Civility, Civility, Trust and the Public Sphere, Society, Individuals and Groups, The necessary transformations of Civil Sphere in the name of Civility

title: Contemporary India
description: Thinking through words: contemporary, India, society/social/association, culture, nation/state-government/region, community/identity/individual Ingredients : religion, caste, tribe, gender, class, race, region, rural/urban, post/colonialism, capitalism/feudalism, modernity/tradition Thematic concerns (an indicative list) How do we transcend caste? Are we modern? Must we be? What is at stake in imagining the nation? Is the call to ‘decolonise the mind’ still relevant? Who is “we the people”? Is mobility possible in Indian society? Whither the Indian village?

title: Notation and Musical Analysis
description: Learning notational representation of pitch/rhythm/tempo/dynamics/instrumentation Identifying keys (major/minor), modes, ragas, intervals and chords Becoming familiar with technical terms required for musical analysis Identifying some basic forms of Western and Indian music Developing aural and verbal skill sets required for conducting stylistic analysis

title: Sociology of Infrastructure
description: What and when is infrastructure? Why does infrastructure matter? Technopolitics of infrastructure Socio-politico processes of infrastructure Suspension and temporalities of infrastructure The promise of infrastructure Infrastructure of injustice, infrastructural injustice and infrastructural deficit region in India.

title: History as a Discipline: Elementary Concepts
description: This course intends to introduce history as a discipline to undergraduate students. While students have read the histories of particular places, times and objects at school (and thus history is not a new subject), they will benefit by learning the ideational processes whereby the study of history got instituted as a compulsory component of school-level education across the world since the nineteenth century. The course is thus a history of ideas that have had a global currency in modern times. Specifically, it delves into the history of the institutionalization of the modern discipline of history. It will probe into why the nineteenth and twentieth century world has often been described as the most historically minded of all ages. In doing so, the course will draw out the differences between the ‘post-Renaissance sense of the past’ and older conceptions of history (the history of Herodotus, Thucydides, for example, or the itihasa-purana traditions of India). A part of the course will, in fact, explore in depth these older traditions. The focus will be on early Indian traditions of engaging with the past—the many facets of itihasa in the long history of the Indian subcontinent. Literary traditions in India go back to more than two millennia: royal biographies, inscriptions, genealogies, chronicles.Thus, the subcontinent had produced numerous texts of recognizable historical intent and value. The term itihasa-purana, referring to that which was believed to have happened in the past,is mentioned in Vedic literature from early to mid-first millennium BC. Itihasa, itivritta, gatha, purana, akhyayika, udaharana, carita, vamsavali, etc. find place of prominence in Brahmanic, Buddhist and Jaina traditions. Jinasena, the ninth century Jaina author of the Adipurana, defines itihasa as relating that which actually happened. There were also changes in the genre of texts associated with itihasa. Besides the itihasa purana traditions, there were the Sramanic and bardic traditions of historical engagement in early India. Comparing with these diverse traditions of historical thinking, the course will familiarize students with how, with the onset of modernity, a rupture was brought about in the dominant definition of history and historical consciousness. This was also the time that history as a formal course of study was introduced into universities (that is, as part of school and college education). A separation was now fastidiously drawn between myth and history; between the supernatural and the natural; and between natural history and human history. As per this distinction, human history was to do only with events consciously actuated and influenced by human-beings. The course has two broad focus areas. One is the process of historical knowledge formation: how the practitioners of the discipline of history reconstruct the past. The other is the exploration of the shifts and turns in the debates on the relation between the natural sciences on the one hand, and humanities and history on the other. The philosophers of the Enlightenment conceived of history as a science—the science of evolution of society. They believed in the ideal of a unified system of all sciences, natural and humane. Another strain of thought, which became predominant in the field of history from the late nineteenth-early-twentieth century, effected a fundamental chasm between human history and the natural sciences. The ‘man-made’ came to be understood as distinctly different from the ‘natural’. The former demanded understanding from the historian, who was supposed to have an inside-view of the matter by entering the minds of the historical actors, while the latter, it was assumed, could be studied only through external observation. beings.The course has two broad focus areas. One is the process of historical knowledge formation: how the practitioners of the discipline of history reconstruct the past. The other is the exploration of the shifts and turns in the debates on the relation between the natural sciences on the one hand, and humanities and history on the other. The philosophers of the Enlightenment conceived of history as a science—the science of evolution of society. They believed in the ideal of a unified system of all sciences, natural and humane. Another strain of thought, which became predominant in the field of history from the late nineteenth-early-twentieth century, effected a fundamental chasm between human history and the natural sciences. The ‘man-made’ came to be understood as distinctly different from the ‘natural’.The former demanded understanding from the historian, who was supposed to have an inside-view of the matter by entering the minds of the historical actors, while the latter, it was assumed,could be studied only through external observation. In the era of Anthropogenic climate change, this distinction gets obfuscated as human action has impacted the biosphere. History thus faces a novel task: its tradition of resolving questions of justice and morality and capacity for empathetic understanding have perhaps to be extended to the entire world of natural reproductive life. The course will benefit students by laying out some basic lines of convergences, unities and tensions between the sciences and the humanities, on the one hand, and between diverse pre-modern/ non-modern sensibilities about the past and a hegemonic modern sensibility, on the other. Content: 1. Unit 1: Pre-modern traditions of engaging with the past. We will focus on the many facets of the itihasa purana traditions in the Indian subcontinent. (See elaboration in the above section.) The module will explore the forms and contents of these genres. 2. Unit 2: The global discourse around history since the nineteenth century. In an era of unprecedented, rapid and dramatic historical transformation that characterized the world since the onset of the Industrial Revolution, history could no longer be simply the model or precedent for the present, as it sometimes functioned before. In this unit, we study the concept of scientific history in the nineteenth century which saw history as laying bare the direction, mechanism and laws of human evolution. Generalized patterns of rise and fall, growth and decay, progress and decline of civilizations; of the evolution of governments and governance, of economic development, etc. were sought to be mapped in the same manner in which geological and biological evolution were being researched. In this unit, we study the concept of empiricism in history—how the supposed assumptions of science were transferred to the social sciences. 3. Unit 2: The historian’s craft—on how methods of writing the past evolved through the turn of the centuries; the processes of condensation, realignment and selection in the reconstruction of the past; the relations between the present and the past; the historian and his facts; the nature of historical evidence, etc. The Collingwoodian concept of the historian as ‘actor’ and not just ‘spectator’—‘entering the minds of another’; imaginative understanding versus explanation; the problem of historical interpretation. 4. Unit 3: “Two cultures”? History and Science—the many debates across the last three centuries over the relations between the study of human history and the study of nature.between the study of human history and the study of nature. 5. Unit 4: Rethinking History in the time of anthropogenic climate change. Certain fundamental ideas about what human-beings can cause/ make or cannot cause/ make have undergone radical revision in the era of global warming.The geophysical agency of human beings is an unprecedented concept. Human history is thus getting re-conceptualized at present. This module focuses of this current rethinking of history. The history of modernity becomes the history of human ecological overreach by grave proportions. The certitudes of human history—of progress, technological mastery over nature, etc.—get unsettled when juxtaposed against the history of natural life on the planet.

title: Understanding the Past
description: 1. Reasoning about the past from the present a) Periodisation (chronology vs process) b) Nature of historical evidence-sources (objectivity/subjectivity debate) 2. Past and its relationship to space and time a) Nation and history b) Maps-Mapping c) Urbanisation-cities 3. Society, politics and culture a) State formation b) Gender c) Movement and migration d) Trade, Crafts and Occupations

title: Social Theory - I
description: The emergence of social theory The sociological imagination The division of labour in society Capitalism and commodification of social relations Religion and social change The total social fact Social Action, its categories and interpretation Organisation of modern society

title: Literary Studies
description: History of Literature, taking English as an example, may be taken up for study in order to understand how the history of English Literature is related to canon-formation. Standard texts on this subject, observe trajectories of tracing the evolution of British literary history. This course will interrogate the trajectory, in ways ranging from accommodating more women authors in the literary history, to re-imagining the conventional subdivision of the writers of each age as major and minor. The course may also look at alternative modes of structuring histories of literature, following departures from diachronic, national-language models, to synchronic models that look at interrelations between different literatures produced in different languages, in which connections emerge because of a multitude of factors (e.g. colonialism, solidarity movements connecting different disenfranchised groups, and so on). For example: 1. European drama—its development seen in synchronic developments across different languages in different nodal points in time (e.g. the Early Modern Period; 19th-century drama, often termed as “Victorian” in the context of English literature; 20th-century interrelations between Indian and European drama); Or, Metaphysical poetry (connected in numerous ways with continental European poetry; with religious emblem books, etc.) Such an approach leads us to reconsider the terms of literary periodisation itself, both in English and in other languages. Questions of periodisation and the social-historical-political are ingrained in how the course examines what ‘literature’ or the ‘literary’ has been imagined to be, how it has been studied in specific points in history, or how nature of such study has shifted over time and space. In this last endeavour, the course is expected to converse with other common courses offered from other disciplines. Topics include: 1.Study of literature by conventional national divisions and periodisation though ‘age’ model: alternative approaches 2The making of the English canon, rethinking the canon, canon formation and values such as questions of literary taste 3 Notions of minor authors and minor literatures, ‘minority voices’ 4 The ways the same text has been read in different places, spaces, and time, sometimes reflexively/relationally/revisionally: for example, William Shakespeare’s The Tempest, Daniel Defoe’s Robinson Crusoe, Charlotte Bronte’s Jane Eyre, Rabindranath Tagore’s The Home and the World, Premchand’s “Sadgati,” U. R. Ananthamurthy’s Samskara 5 Synchronic and diachronic literary exchanges that have reinforced or disrupted canons and canonicity: translations, adaptations, appropriations, collaborations

title: Introduction to Theatre and Performance Studies
description: 1.The main objective of the course is to open up the field of ‘Theatre and Performance Studies’ to the students such that the various debates around it across continents become clear to them and it enables thinking about the discipline within our contemporary academic landscape. 2 The course would broadly familiarise students with the interactions between ‘Theatre and Performance Studies’ to other disciplines from which it has drawn extensively vis-à-vis concepts such as aesthetic experience, play, practice, sadhana, theatrum philosophicum, theatricality, rasa, dhvani, mimesis, performativity, mediality, corporeality etc. 3. It would familiarise students with research methodologies such as fieldwork and ethnography, performance as research (PAR) in the study of Theatre and/or Performance such as the works of Victor Turner, Richard Schechner, Eugenio Barba, Dwight Conquergood, Baz Kershaw, and others. 4. A part of the course would be devoted to methods of documentation and archiving processes/techniques that are implemented in documenting theatre and/or performance, so that students are aware of the various mechanisms of documentation followed in archives of the performing arts and the role they play in the study of theatre and/or performance, theatre historiography and performance making.

title: Research Methods: Expository Writing & Analysis
description: The course will require students to carry out regular written work related to the material inthe theory courses, with peer and instructor feedback. The aims of the course are to help thestudent to analyse take the preliminary steps in academic writing through their readingsand to help them express ideas accurately, concisely, and with clarity. The tasks will coverpre-writing plans, organisation of the ideas to be expressed, structuring, attending toviewpoints and audience, precision in language use, recognising their individual style, andadjusting to practical constraints such as word-limits, editorial restrictions, deadlines etc.

title: Research Methods: Critical Reading
description: Research papers in the areas touched upon by the theory components of this programmewill be read, analysed and discussed. For example, a paper on periodisation that recasts thestandard canon would show that the canon is a construct.Selected portions of the literary history and texts could be read either revisionally orreflexively, tracing it over time, thus bringing the critical analytical component in. Forinstance, for a very long time, interpretations of William Shakespeare`s The Tempestreading of Prospero as the civilising influence on the native Caliban endured. In the last fewdecades, the robust development of postcolonial (re)reading of this play questioned thisparadigm could be tracked. The choice of the texts, for such scrutiny/case study, may vary from term to term, the example of The Tempest being suggestive rather than definitive.Tasks that students may be assigned to do include: read and produce a critical bibliography of a certain number of published researchpapers in a specific area1 select a seed text and two-three divergent/revisionary readings of this divided bylocation (temporal, geographical, political)2 survey syllabi/curriculum/course-outlines of how various departments, spread overtime and space, teach literature, or survey anthologies in a specific area published over aperiod of time.

title: Research Methods: Qualitative Methods
description: 1. Introduction to Qualitative Methods2. Ethnography 302226 Participant and Non-Participant3. Grounded Theory, Narrative Analysis4. Mixed methods and Ethnography5. Hands-on project on Ethnography.

title: Research Methods : Sources and Methods and the Past
description: a) Reasoning about the past from the presenti) Periodisation (chronology vs process)ii) Nature of historical evidence-sources (objectivity/subjectivity debate)iii) Kinds of sources: archives, texts, oral histories, material remains; disciplines-arthistory, history, archaeologyb) Past and its relationship to space and timei) Nation and historyii) Maps-Mappingiii) Urbanisation-citiesc) Society, politics and culturei) When was the source produced?ii) Who produced the source?iii) For whom?

title: Performance Historiography and Documentation
description: Part I: This course would be devoted toi) understanding the debates around ephemerality and the archive andii) how in the context of these debates the archive is employed in theatre historiographyA part of the course will be devoted to examples and techniques that have been developed inemploying history or the archive in performance making.Part II:The course will be also enable a detailed understanding of methods of documentation andarchiving processes/techniques that are implemented in documenting theatre and/orperformance, so that students are aware of the various mechanisms of documentationfollowed in archives of the performing arts and the role they play in the study of theatreand/or performance. This section will require students to actively participate in internshipsat theatre and performance archives.Practicum: The course will require students to do an internship in a theatre archive such asNCPA library, Natyashodh, Maharashtra Cultural Centre, Ashok Da Ranade Archives, Archivesand research centre for ethnomusicology etc.

title: Literary Periodisation
description: The course is imagined to have three unequal components.  Part A: A quarter of the course to be devoted to overview of periodisation and the history/ies and contours of literature that it yields, so that students are broadly familiar with the various periods as named in English literary studies: Early Modern, Neo- classical, Romantic, Victorian, Modern, and so on.  Part B: A large part of the course will be devoted to studying one or two period/s as representative “case/s”. Instructor/s will choose the period/s for study and determine the organisation of the course content. The selection will aim to (i) frame the selected period in terms of dis/continuities with other periods and trace the historical trajectories and what would typically be seen as “evolution” of literature, (ii) animate the sources/texts/writers/debates/research fields that have emerged in the study of the period. Part C: A brief concluding segment will critically re-address the issue of periodisation in literary studies. Based on the period/s studied in the course, units could be on (i) the possibilities and problems of studying Literature as discrete periods. (ii) how the configuration and nomenclatures of periods are being continually debated (examples: Elizabethan Age vs. Renaissance vs. Early Modern Period’; Victorian Age vs. the Age of the Empire), by asking questions such as when was women’s ‘Renaissance’, or “When was Modernism?” in locations other than in the Anglo-American, and so on

title: Genre and Literature
description: The focus of this course is on studying one, possibly two genres with a view towards:  Showing the emergence, trajectory and variabilities of the genre studied. Thus, for example, if Tragedy is the chosen genre, then how has this dramatic form manifested itself in text and on stage since the period of Greek Tragedy. Taking for scrutiny the formal features of the genre studied: With the help of both theoretical formulations on the genre, and textual examples Situating the concerned genre in relation to other genres, and in the process, scrutinise adaptations, transactions, boundary-formations, and circulations, such as for instance, Anthony Burgess` novel A Clockwork Orange (1962) and Stanley Kubrick`s adaptation of it in the film A Clockwork Orange (1971) Demonstrating how different literary periods, movements and schools have centred genre differently: such as Formalism, New Criticism, Structuralism, Feminism, New Historicism etc.

title: Literary Theory
description: The course will take a survey form, aiming to introduce students to the history of engagement with literary (and other artistic) forms. As a result, the course, while allowing for conversations across time, will take the route of thinking in linear time to examine developments of thought regarding literature. In doing so, the limits of such linear imagination would also be exposed. It will take the crystallisation of ‘Literature’ as an autonomous domain as the ground to examine how a canon was formed over time. Possible modules listed below.  What is Literature? Historical formation of an autonomous domain for disciplinary and critical consideration; ‘critique’ and ‘imagination’ in determination of the human subject, beginning with the philosophical writings of Immanuel Kant and made central by the literary critical efforts by the Romantics; traditions in organising criticism/theory including chronological, or based on ideological or historical co-incidences; thinking with and through enduring debates, "schools", convergences  Aesthetics and Ethics Theories of art making in antiquity with an attention to writings in the Greek, Perso-Arabic and Sanskrit traditions, focusing of different conceptual and classificatory practices  Authors, Genres, Traditions Career of the concept of the author, genre and literary traditions, ranging from Samuel Johnson’s Lives of the Poets, author-centric critical practices and concepts such as “intentional fallacy,” and re-consideration of the author figure; development of canons around authors and literary traditions modelled by and around Mathew Arnold and TS Eliot among, others; the emergence of the ‘lyric’ and later the novel as units of analysis in the context of debates around “literary fact” (Mary Poovey)  Language, Form and Close Reading Centralisation of the practice of language as constituting the central thematic for literary criticism/theory, with the emergence of ‘New Criticism’, ‘Practical Criticism’ and Russian Formalism, and structuralist linguistics and anthropology on the other hand; further development of and response to these debates by scholars of post-structuralist and narratological persuasions  The Material Foundations of Literature Critical thinking that framed literature in relation to material and infrastructural foundations through Marxism and psychoanalysis; “hermeneutics of suspicion” (Paul Ricouer)  What Literature? Challenges to the boundary-making practices ingrained in the concept of literature as it emerged in the Anglo-American tradition of literary criticism /theory; shifts occasioned by political impulses and/or geo-political considerations: challenges from Feminist theory, race theory, postcolonial theory, or theories and reading practices grappling with issues of sexuality, ethnicity and caste; translation and the emergence of the discipline of Comparative Literature and the field of ‘World Literature’  Literature and other fields/disciplines The question of autonomy of literature and its relationship with other fields and disciplines: relationship of literature with disciplines such as history, anthropology and sociology, which has been theorised extensively, and the natural sciences (especially with a focus on the environment), medicine and the law, more recently; the exchanges between the literary and the popular  Post-critique The “post-critical” (from the late 1990s and as loosely called): return to the history of the formation of the discipline and its assumptions, in face of the challenges that the discipline faced as discussed in the previous module, such as how literature came to be imagined as providing knowledge and attempts to re-state the salience of the ‘literature’; historicisation of literary criticism as a dynamic space that needs re-imagination and re-invention; methodologies developed in light of the digital turn or re-animation of newer textual reading practices

title: Shakespeare`s Plays and their Afterlives
description:  Topics will include an overview of Shakespearean theatre, audience, classification of his plays, and the main corpus of significant Shakespearean criticism down the centuries. This course will close-read at least 3 or 4 Shakespearean plays and their more famous re-inventions after the sixteenth century.  Genre could be one parameter of selecting the texts, taking a cue from the classifications of the Shakespearean canon. To take a few examples:  Shakespeare`s Scottish play will be read in sufficient detail and re-considered in the light of its afterlives. These afterlives could span a wide linguistic and cultural range, from Orson Welles` Voodoo Macbeth and Roman Polanski`s The Tragedy of Macbeth to Akira Kurosawa`s Throne of Blood and Vishal Bharadwaj`s Maqbool.  Similarly, an early comedy like A Midsummer Night`s Dream could be read alongside Neil Gaiman`s graphic novel "A Midsummer Night`s Dream" and Habib Tanvir`s Chhattisgarhi recreation Kamdev ka Apna, Basant Ritu ka Sapna; or a later and mature comedy like Twelfth Night read alongside Atul Kumar`s Piya Behrupiya.  A significant objective of this reading is for comparative analysis, as well as an understanding of how themes and techniques come to be modified and re-envisioned, not just across cultural contexts but also genres and media, like the graphic novel and film. Questions arising from these correspondences and ranging from the generic to the specific, will find special place in this course: a) How does a pre-eminently visual medium such as Neil Gaiman’s graphic-novel version of A Midsummer Night’s Dream speak to a play, whose language was seen as a competent substitute for visual accessories including stage props? b) How, to what extent and towards what intent do Shakespearean rewritings exploit the formulaic nature of his plays? c) What interpretative dimension has technology added to reinventions? d) What is the readership/audience projected by the afterlives? e) How do the plays and their afterlives negotiate with literary, technical and political self-reflexivity? How are creative opportunities provided and identified in the Shakespearean canon?

title: Author, Authorship and Authority
description: Topics of study may seek to examine  Canon formations, Award-regimes  Socio-economic, cultural, political and legal conditions that produce notions of author/ship in different periods and places (e.g.: rise of the professional author copyright laws, the rise/fall of magazine culture, reading cultures)  Minority writing/Minor literatures  Literary controversies  “Anonymous”, Pseudonyms, Heteronyms,  Individual careers In this course we would read a variety of texts ranging from  letters, speeches, award citations, prefaces, manifestoes, auto/biographies  literary pieces that centre the figure of the author and/or issues of authorship (e.g.: Alexander. Pope, “The Dunciad”; Thomas Chatterton, Poems supposed to have been written at Bristol in the Fifteenth Century by Thomas Rowley, Priest, &c.”; A.S Byatt, Possession; Alison Bechdel, Are You My Mother?; Doris Lessing, The Golden Notebook; Roberto Bolano Amulet  works of literary criticism and theory

title: Social Theory - II
description:  Action, structure, function and agency  Self and other  The social construction of reality  Structuralism in sociology / anthropology  The interpretation of cultures  Ethnomethodology and Phenomenology  Feminism and the subversion of identity

title: Indian Society
description:  Social Inequality and Social Stratification in India: Caste, Tribe, Class (Urban and Rural, Agrarian, Middle Class), Ethnicity)  Family and Kinship: Institutions and Transformation  Gender and Patriarchy: Honour, Violence, and Intersectionality  Agrarian Change and Urbanism in India: Persisting and Emerging Inequalities, Opportunities for Mobility  Social Mobility: Trends, Possibilities, Policies  Social Movements and Social Conflicts in India: Women, Students, Peasants and Farmers, Working Class, Region and Ethnicity, Environment  Religion and Faith in India: Religiosity, Materiality and New Spiritual Movements  Secularism, Communalism, and Nationalism: Challenges of Diversity and Pluralism  Demography and Human Development: Issues, Patterns, and Trajectories

title: Tribal Studies: Concepts and Perspective
description:  Concepts and perspectives on Tribes  Theoretical approaches to tribes  Tribe-Caste continuum debate  Social transformation and tribal identity  State policies and Tribes  Political economy of tribal development  Towards an alternative epistemology of tribes

title: Sociology of Development
description: 1. Genealogy of the concept of Development: tracing how ideas concerning development have evolved along with various stages of growth of capitalism- starting from classical political economy to present times. 2. Modernisation theory (Rostow, Parsons, Lerner, McClelland, Hoselitz) and its critique 3. Marxist and Neo-Marxist theories of Development: Historical materialism (Marx), Dependency theories and World Systems approach (Baran, Frank, Wallerstein, Samir Amin), Mode of production debate (Laclau, Alavi, Rey) 4. Feminist theories of Development: Women in Development (WID), Women and Development (WAD), Gender and Development (GAD), Women, Environment and Development (WED), Postmodernism and Development (PAD). 5. Postdevelopmentalism and its critique: Arturo Escobar (Encountering Development), Esteva, Sachs (Development Dictionary), Rahnema, Claude Alvares. Its Critique (Jan Nederveen Pieterse, Aram Ziai) 6. 6. Other approaches: Development as Freedom and Capabilities approach (Amartya Sen and Martha Nussbaum)

title: Sociology of Gender
description:  Introduction – what is gender anyway?  Doing gender .... And race and class  Sexuality, sexual identity  Masculinities  Gender, science, and research  Explaining gender inequality (India and global)  Gender and culture (India and global)  The gendered division of labor  The organisation of gender  Gender, politics and the state  Gender and globalisation  Feminist organising, gender democracy and gender mainstreaming: a global perspective  Ecofeminism  Gender-based rights, social movements (India, global south and global north)

title: Quantitative Methods in Social Sciences
description: With emphasis on quantitative methods, the students will learn the basics of research design, data collection and analysis, and application of the concepts and tools of statistics in social science research.  The first module will discuss the theory underpinning the design of quantitative research. Basics of conceptualisation of enquiry, operationalisation, measurement and sampling, inference and causality using techniques like survey methods.  The second module will discuss descriptive statistics, including measures of central tendency and dispersion.  The third module will deal with the fundamentals of inferential statistics, including probability distributions, hypothesis testing, analysis of variance and regression analysis. Program and use ‘R’ and other software for statistical analysis. The fourth module will discuss how to use publicly available digital data resources (viz. census data, NSSO) for empirical social research. The fifth module will discuss how to use social media digital data resources (viz. Twitter) for empirical social research.

title: Studying Literature
description: The course introduces the objects, methods, debates and critical frames of literary studies. The course will centre for study: • a selection of texts in a range of forms and genres (fiction, poetry, prose, songs, travel- writing, graphic novels, life writing, plays, and so on) • various constituents of studying the literary (Author, Reader, Text, World) and the changing relations between them • methods of reading within the discipline (close reading, critical reading, surface reading, distant reading, and so on) Through these, the course also hopes to open out several of the conversations and debates within literary studies and sometimes in its interface with other disciplines. These may include, but need not be confined to: • The contours of the literary • Canons and counter-canons • Modes of contextualization • Historical shaping of the discipline • Critical approaches of framing and studying literature (new critical, structuralist, new historical, adaptation studies, feminist, psychoanalyst, Marxist, narratological) • Interdisciplinarity: crossover with ethnographic, archival, historical, stylistic approaches • Literary studies and Human Sciences • Socio-historical-cultural-intellectual contexts of the “literary” (institutions, infrastructures, media, ideologies, circulations-translations-adaptations).

title: Studying Performance
description: This course introduces students to the idea of performance and its pedagogic practices. The course equips students with critical skills to understand performance, aesthetics and virtuosity and will familiarize students with the performed practice and theories of theatre, dance and other related art forms and also borrow conceptually from the field to understand other social and ritualistic phenomena. Understanding these concepts will help students to focus on the nuances of performance and other related dynamic processes discussed within, helping the student to develop critical engagement and understanding of the world of Performance Studies. It will include viewing various performances in person, artist talks and workshops discussing its practice, history and the performers unfolding investigations, thoughtful conversations and diverse engagements and perspectives.

title: Studying Media
description: The proposed course introduces students to the foundations of media studies as a field of research, pedagogy, and practice. The course explores a humanities-oriented approach to media where media becomes an operative term to bring together a wide array of disciplines like film studies, visual cultures, digital cultures, television studies, and media archaeology. It trains students to critically and effectively engage with key concepts, approaches, and debates in the field that focuses on: • Media, culture and society • Popular Culture and Media • Film and Media theory • Practices of looking • Introduction to Media through the senses • Form, genres and movements • Media infrastructures • Cinema decades • Production, distribution and circulation • Archive • Stardom and fandom

title: Historicising Society, State and Culture
description: 1. Political history: state, law, war, ideas, movements. 2. Economic history: trade, industry, labour, agriculture, technology. 3. Social history: gender, caste, class, education. 4. Cultural history: art, performance, painting, photography, language, literature. 5. Environmental history: land, forests, water, people.

title: From the ground up: Basics of History and Archaeology
description: 1. How material remains help us understand the past. 2. Sources of understanding the past: relating texts to material sources 3. Relationship between the past and present 4. Evidence, Expertise and Reasoning in researching the past 5. Nature and culture or Humans and their environment 6. Culture in archaeology: why and how it is important 7. Time and space 8. Modernity and Deep time 9. Defining sites and landscapes 10. Social structures focusing on the premodern 11. Civilizations and Empires

title: Affective Neuroscience
description: Basic and advanced theories of emotions in the perspective of neuroscience, the difference between emotions, mood, and feelings, neural mechanisms underlying the interaction of emotional processes with cognitive processes such as attention, learning, memory, and decision- making, neuroimaging methods to measure emotions, emotion regulation and coping strategies, how the scope of attention modulates the perception of emotional information and vice versa, the role of positive emotions in our life, how emotional information can be incorporated in the human-computer interaction.

title: Fundamentals of Language Science
description: The course aims to do the following: 1. Situate language in the intersection of various disciplines to show how an understanding of the nature of human language is arrived at through a cross- disciplinary mode of inquiry and to also show how a study of language drives understanding and inquiry within other disciplines as well. 2. Survey the scientific methods and tools that allow an understanding of a. the general phenomenon of language b. the cross-linguistic variation in human languages and the abstract cognitive capacity for language

title: Sociology of Health and Medicine
description: The course is divided into five modules: • Module one will provide an overview of the sociology of health and medicine (SOHM) using case studies that utilize sociological, anthropological, and historical approaches. The module will also introduce major theoretical approaches and theories in sociology for understanding health and medicine in contemporary times. • Module two will engage with major anthropological contributions to the study of health, culture, and medicine. Furthermore, adapting a historical perspective, the module will throw light on how the medical institutions of India were shaped by colonial medicine and the colonial state, and continuation of medical pluralism in India. • Module three will focus on debates about public health in India, first by engaging with policy interventions of the state after independence, and subsequent changes to health policy post liberalization. • Module four will focus on consequent changes brought about by the participation of the private sector in various medical functions, ongoing urbanization, issues of climate change, and debates about increasing use of technology driven solutions including digital health for public health. • Module five will focus on health inequalities in India and its correlation with dimensions of social stratification including poverty, gender, class, caste, region, religion etc.

title: The Philosophy of Shabdayoga
description: Nature of Shabda, Nature of Artha, nature of the relation of Shabda, Artha and cognition. The concept of Arthakasha and Shabdakasha, Rasa and the Shabda, bhava, expression of the Rasa and the Shabda, Cognitive Maze, Cognitive dispersal, Cognitive Unity What is Alamkara? Types of alamkaras, shabda-alamkaras and artha-alamkaras, the concept of Pratibha, the concept of Chamatkrti, Cognitive friction Shabdayoga as the main theme of Bhagavadgita, karmayoga, bhaktiyoga and other yogas and how different they are from Shabdayoga,

title: Feminist Interventions: Texts, Frames, Politics
description: By attending to literary productions and bringing them in conversation with inter/disciplinary explorations, this course will examine a range of feminist interventions in select topics/areas. In each iteration of the course, the instructor will choose focal nodes and themes for rigorous exploration. These could include, but are not restricted to, feminist interventions and debates on (particular) institutions, values, figurations, aesthetics, narratives, things, affects, relations, time-space. A representative version of the course may bring together the followingliterary texts: Charlotte Gilman`s short story "The Yellow Wallpaper", Saadat Manto`s "Toba Tek Singh", Toni Morrison`s Beloved, Jean Rhys` Wide Sargossa Sea, C. Ayyappan`s short story "Madness" along with a range of critical-theoretical texts. Through a reading of these and supporting scholarship, the course would tease out: 1) histories of feminist studies 2) the place of the literary in feminist knowledge-making 3) how feminist scholarship and contentions unsettle and re-form ideas of i) madness and rationality ii) domesticity iii) nationalism iv) history-telling and remembering iv)"experience" v) form and genre

title: Climate Change and the Environment: Scientific and Literary Perspectives
description: Climate change—tracking, measurement, impact; Mitigating/adaptation to climate change—scientific and humanistic perspectives; Environmental Literature and Climate Fiction—relationship with scientific knowledge; Environmental issues, public understanding, and the media— the roles of reports, fiction, documentaries, and other modes of communication; Climate change and intergenerational responsibility— perspectives from the humanities.

title: Dance History and Appreciation
description: The course contents areThe module starts on introducing the concepts of Dance and its Origins, theconcepts of Rhythm and Movement, what becomes the Material of Danceand when does a movement becomes Dance and becomes an performativeArtThe next module with focus on the concept of dance which foregrounds onhow ethnic-cultural, and ritual dances impact and reflect society, the coursewill focus on the methods and the theoretical dimensions with exploringvarious dance forms from both global and local.The next module will help students to predominately concentrate on howdance is viewed within the social and historical context and its impact in theOrient and the Occident, how to understand the dance forms in historicalcontext and segregate them in certain parameters to understand the danceform, the dancing culture and the dancer. One among of the parameter willbe the presentation (trained dance forms through guru/institutional/academysuch classical dance forms) the participation (the community dance formswhere the learning pf the practice is through by being part of festivals andceremonies) dance forms in IndiaThe course will have a module where there will be viewing of specificperformances and discussing the earlier concepts which was introduced inthe earlier sections, the different dance forms could be such as Ballet,Capoeira, Pow wow, Hip Hop, Cambodian classical dance etc.The next module will have a similar method of viewing of specificperformances and discussing the earlier concepts with dance forms from India such as Bharatnatyam, Odissi, Kathakali, Chhau, Indian Reginal dance forms etc. the focus will also be in sections such as the Indian Perspective on the Origins and definition of dance, the nature of dance forms, the history of certain dance forms, the Post-colonial nature of dance forms the course aids in understanding them through history and in terms of their techniques as well as practice. the course will also differentiate with the concepts of classical, folk, ritual, rites, community dance etc.The course will have sections as what are dance forms, origins of it, the power of dance forms by understanding the historical changes and continuation of the art form and exploring certain movement practices which also reflects some balancing and rhythm.Each class will have an elaborative presentation on dance forms and related videos and discussion. This course can help the students in understanding dance and its aesthetics while watching any dance forms.

title: Field Visit
description: x

title: Communication and Presentation Skills
description: The aim of this course is to equip the post-graduate students with basic communication and presentation skills for academic and professional purposes. Remedial work will be conducted wherever necessary. The course will focus on the following topics: The process of communication; barriers to communication and how they can be overcome. Types of communication: verbal and non-verbal. Non-verbal communication: body language Verbal communication: Oral and Written Oral: elements of pronunciation, oral presentation, group discussion Written: technical reports, business letters Reception skills: reading and listening skills Vocabulary and Grammar Style and Usage: Punctuation, Readability and Culture-sensitivity Use of computerized audio-visual aids for academic and professional presentations. Psychological and Sociological Aspects of communication

title: Applied Economics
description: Introduction: Nature and Scope of Applied Economics ? Methodological Aspects ? Common Tools and Techniques. Economics of Risk and Uncertainty ? Difference between Applied Economics and Economics. Fundamental concepts associated with Applied Economics. Analysis of Demand and Supply and their Applications: Elasticity of Demand and Supply, and Applications to major Economic Issues. Demand Estimation and Forecasting. Study of Economic Environment. Theory of the firm ? Objectives ? Production and Cost Analysis ? Production Function ? Efficiency and Productivity ? Decision making under Uncertainty ? The value of Information. Various definitions of Costs for Managerial Applications ? Cost Estimation and Forecasting. Market Structure & Pricing and Output Strategy: Perfect Competition, Monopoly, Monopolistic Competition and Oligopoly ? Game Theory and Competitive Strategy. Fundamental Elements of Investment Analysis.

title: Development, Technology and Global Order
description: Development as a global process: Change from nationally organized social and economic order to globally organized pattern- Emerging Socio-Spatial networks of social interaction in the world today: Technology and the transformation in the International order; New transnational civil society and new world order. Globalization as an emergent concept: Rise of the development project, formation of coalition building and developmentalist alliances: Global production system and infrastructural dimension, global agribusiness and global regionalism across center- periphery boundaries; Freedom of enterprise under GATT regime, WTO as an integrated dispute settlement mechanism, Globalization of knowledge and politics of global intellectual property. Technology and the power of e-governance: Globalization of telecommunications and the issue of Regulatory reform; Globalization and national sovereignty, Internet media and alternative sources of information: Change in the quality of democratic participation, electronic democracy and civic networking movement; e-democracy and criteria in Participatory Technology Decisions, tackling the problems of digital divide. Social responses to globalization: Fundamentalism, Environmentalism, New Social Movement and alternative modes of development: Policies and Issues in Environment and Development: Poverty and global development, Feminism and feminist formulation in development-- from WID to GAD, multiculturalism and consmopolitan localism: Structural characteristics of late modernity- `From Emancipatory to Llife Politics?.

title: Law, Governance, Rights and Development
description: The main objective of the course is to introduce students to the concepts of rights and governance and relate these to current debates on justice and development. This will include understanding in a historical perspective the important role of law in social, political, and economic development. The course will provide students with an understanding of the governance and development debates. It will enable them to understand the importance of laws, policies, social capital and civic participation for improving the effectiveness, accountability and legitimacy of governance for development. The course will also enhance capacity of students to develop and implement policies in public and private and NGO organizational environments.The major topics to be covered include:1.011Concepts: Rights, Justice, Citizenship, developmental state2.011Relationship between Civil society, state, governance and development 3.011Law, legal evolution and social change4.011States, markets and governance5.011Law, inequalities, and governance 6.011The emergence of the 221good governance222 debate: Democratization, participation, social capital, and decentralization.7.011Issues related to accountability, and ethics8.011The construction of 221effective222 states (e.g. policy-making capacity, civil service reform, , legal processes and the rule of law) 9.011Human rights issues, and governance for development10.011Social movements, NGOS, and development advocacy

title: Mind and Machine : Experimental Psychology for Tech and Innovation
description: Through this course, students will gain insights into designing technology that aligns with human cognitive abilities and limitations by engaging students in psychological principles that reveal how users think, perceive, and interact. Understanding experimental psychology enables students to create technologies that are intuitive, effective, and ethically aligned with human needs. Students will learn how these principles can be applied to designing and developing user-centred technologies. Skills in user testing, perceptual feedback, and cognitive load management become vital tools in creating responsive and adaptive technology solutions. Emphasizing scientific rigour and user empathy, the course prepares students to innovate technology that aligns with human cognitive needs, enhancing user experience and engagement.Module 1: Foundations of Experimental Psychology * Introduction to Experimental Psychology * Fundamental Cognitive Processes: Perception, Attention, Memory, Decision-Making * Scientific Method in Psychology: Hypothesis, Operational Definitions, Controls Module 2: Interaction between Human Cognition and Technology * Mental Models and Human-Computer Interaction * Cognitive Architecture and Information Processing * Cognitive Load Theory and Technology Design Module 3: Perception and Interface Design * Visual and Auditory Perception in Interface Design * Principles of Gestalt Psychology in UX/UI Module 4: Attention and Usability * Selective and Divided Attention * Designing for Focus: Avoiding Cognitive Overload Module 5: Memory, Learning, and User Retention * Working Memory Constraints and Interface Design * Long-term Memory, Learning Curves, and Habit Formation in Tech Use Module 6: Emotion, Motivation, and User Engagement * Affective Computing: Emotion-Aware Systems * Motivation, Flow, and Engagement in Interactive Technologies * Ethical Considerations in Behavioral Design * Emotion and human-computer interaction Module 7: Ethics and Human-Centered Innovation * Psychological Impacts of Technology * Designing with Empathy and Responsibility: Ethical Boundaries in Design

title: Engendering Development Theory and Practice
description: Since 1975, feminist theory has had an impact on the development discourse. In India too, the women222s movement and feminist scholarship has intervened in critical issues related to labour, sexuality, caste, environment, impact of technology and law and politics. Gender has been established as a crucial component of all development programmes and strategies. In this context, this course seeks to introduce the participants to:1.Contemporary theoretical debates in Gender and Development.2.Feminist interventions in the institutional framework of development thought and practice in India.3.Women222s struggles for resistance and strategies for empowerment. This course, through the use of case material will train students to analyse the interactions between ideas and practices in relation to specific cases and examples of development interventions.1.011 Debates on Gender and Development: A Background (FromWID to GAD, the Women, Development and Environment School, The Dual strategies framework)2.011Third World centered Feminism and critiques of development (Dislocating cultures, Re-orienting Western feminism, Postcolonial feminist critiques of Development, Feminisms and Internationalisms)3.011Analytical concepts in the institutional framework of development; Gender, patriarchy, sexual division of labour, production and reproduction4.011Understanding the institutional framework of gender and development in India: Gender, caste and Kinship, household and labour, State policies and programmes, gendered poverty.5.011Women222s movements of resistance: Understanding the contemporary women222s movement, the spaces made by women in other emancipatory struggles, NGOisation of the women222s movement and its implications.6.011Strategies for empowerment: The concept of empowerment, case studies of SEWA, Ragpickers and Domestic workers Union. Gender training frameworks in state and non-governmental organisations.

title: Historical Linguistics
description: Introduction to Diachronic Linguistics, comparative method Genetic relationship, grouping, reconstruction, language family theory of sound change, syntactic and semantic change, change in progress sound change and analogy, reorganization of phonemic inventory of a language Computational methods to assist in compiling, comparing and mapping of language development

title: Classical Indian Thought in Modern Times
Empirical Analysis of Corporate Governance
description: ▪ Overview of the Empirical method to address Endogeneity o Ordinary least square o Limited dependent variable o Instrument variable o Estimation methods for longitudinal data o Control function estimation ▪ Overview of the Corporation and Corporate Governance o Financing modes and agency conflict. o Internal Corporate governance mechanism o External corporate governance mechanism ▪ Ownership structure corporate governance Issues o Opportunism of controlling shareholders o Type-I and Type-II agency cost o Measurement of Type-I and Type-II agency Cost ▪ Effect of corporate governance and ownership on o Firm’s performance o Firm’s accounting practices o Firm policies like taxation, cash holding etc o Merger and Acquisition o Executive compensation ▪ Corporate governance and stakeholder management o Firms should optimize the stakeholder or the shareholder’s return o Relationship between firm’s financial and firm’s social performance o Drivers of firm’s Corporate Social Responsibility and its effect on firm’s policy

title: Philosophy of Jñānaśabdavāda
description: ntroduction to the theories of : (i) Jāna-śabda-vāda, (ii) sarva-satya-vāda, (iii) śabda-yoga-vāda Nature of śabda, Nature of Artha, śabda as a Pramana, śabda and Cognition, Nature of cognition, Orality, what is Omnilinguality? How is it theoretically possible? Compositionality of śabda and Artha, Process of speech production, Sangraha being the sentence meaning, śabdayoga: the essence of Gita, Nature of Yogyatā between śabdas as well as Arthas, Nature of prapacha and its creation, existence and destruction

title: Research Project -I
description: For MARes programme

title: Research Project -II
description: For MARes Programme

title: Methods: Language, Text, and the Performative
description: This course on methods as applied to Language, Text and the Performative will allow students to 1. Apply disciplinary concepts 2. Understand and use analytical tools from at least two (if not all three) disciplines of Linguistics, Literature, and Performance to an original work by applying them to at least two themes. The following is an example; the themes/keywords may change given the instructors and class profiles. Medium: In Linguistics, what is medium/channels for language or communication; how do the channels shape linguistic structures, meaning, and transmission; key considerations include Orality vs. Literacy; impact of recording, digital tools, and online platforms; the role of gestures, visuals, and other non-verbal elements; language standardization, education. In Literary Studies, we ask questions such as how the medium and its mediations affect our reading practices and analytical protocols. This course will offer exercises in reading texts across media such as print, oral narratives, and film & digital media, among others. Performance Studies has more recently been studied within the framework of Critical Media Studies because of the role performance plays as a medium and its necessary relationship to materiality, process, and reception. Other keywords that may be used include: Structure and form; knowledge; innateness; community; canon-canonical; narration; historiography, etc.

title: Language, Text, and the Performative
description: This course investigates the theoretical and practical connections between Linguistics, Literature, and Performance Studies, with a focus on the reciprocal influence of at least two (if not all three) of these fields. We will examine the ways in which language, text, and performance operate within various cultural contexts, including, but not limited to, everyday discourse, literary forms, theatrical performance, oral traditions, and ritual practices. Students will develop a sophisticated understanding of the processes of meaning-making, the power of expression, and the construction of cultural productions. ** Course Objectives:Students are expected to learn to: 1. Understand the nature and structure of language, what linguistic analysis means, the difference between internal(i)-language vs external (e)-language. 2. Interpret and analyse literary texts and fields. 3. Explore the performative, including that of the body and its praxis/practice, and their relationship to the textual or non-textual practices. 4. Understand and apply analytical principles to an original work or a conjoined object of study.Modules: ** Foundations of Language - The nature of language: structure, and functions - Language in context, conversation, discourse, narration - Oral and written language - Language as a lens when analyzing the performative and the text. **Literary Fields - Close reading and other ways of analysing literary texts - Examining the dynamic constitution of literary fields - the changing conceptions of the literary, audiences, publics, readership, authorship, adaptations, etc. - The forms and genres of the literary. ** Performative Practices - Exploring concepts in Performance Studies - Dramaturgy - Performance ethnography and Performance historiography - Understanding the context within which Performance as Research emerged.

title: Academic Writing & Analysis
description: The course will explore the following themes and questions: * Knowledge production in the Humanities and Social Sciences * Heuristics, Methods of Discovery and Problem Formulation * Modes of Reasoning: Their limits and Possibilities * What can Interpretation do? * On Text, Sample, Field, Archive, Source, Performance * Claim-Making, Evidencing and Argumentation * Writing Culture, History, the Social It will also train students to bring these concerns into their writing. The course will also, therefore, cover pre-writing plans, organisation of the ideas to be expressed, structuring, attending to viewpoints and audience, precision in language use, recognising their individual style, and adjusting to practical constraints such as word-limits, editorial restrictions, deadlines etc

timetable.professor: Rachel A. Varghese, Sharmila Sreekumar, Suryakant Waghmore

title: Humans and Landscapes across Deep Time
description: The course will have three core sections: 1. Archaeology, Landscapes and the Human Past : Archaeology has long been exploring ways of understanding human-land interactions. While earlier work concentrated on how humans over time have exploited the resources on and off the landscapes, later research tried to better understand how humans perceived the landscape. This part of the course will outline some of the different perspectives in order to understand the relationship between humans, the landscape and technological changes over time. 2. Paleobiological studies and the Landscape : This segment explores how paleobiological evidence - from fossil pollen to ancient animal remains - helps reconstruct past landscapes and ecosystems.
